prompt: Match each stakeholder behaviour to the engagement strategy that addresses its ROOT CAUSE. Two strategies don't fit — leave them in the tray.

rows.text: 'Middle manager is blocking progress — fears losing authority'
'Technical team has gone quiet and stopped offering ideas'
'Executive sponsor has missed the last three steering meetings'
'Frontline users are asking to transfer to avoid the new system'

answers.why: 'Blocking from fear of lost authority → a private session to surface concerns and give them a role.'
'Disengaged experts re-engage when their contributions are visible and valued.'
'A busy sponsor needs short, benefits-focused visibility through existing forums.'
'Users avoiding the system need capability building, not pressure.'
'Heavy-handed — forcing compliance rarely fixes the root cause of resistance.'
'Avoidant — dropping deliverables to dodge resistance is not engagement.'

explanation: Tailor engagement to the ROOT CAUSE: private alignment for fear-based blocking, recognition for disengaged experts, concise benefits for a busy sponsor, and coaching for users lacking capability. Directives and scope cuts don't address the cause.

prompt: Match each risk situation to the most appropriate response strategy. One strategy doesn't fit — leave it in the tray.

rows.text: 'A planned feature would breach a new regulation'
'A key specialist may leave part-way through the project'
'Risk of fire or theft at the off-site equipment store'
'A high-impact event that is very unlikely and cannot be prevented'

answers.why: 'You cannot run a non-compliant feature — eliminate the risk by removing or changing it.'
'Reduce the impact — cross-train and document so losing the specialist hurts less.'
'Shift the financial impact to a third party via insurance.'
'Cannot prevent it and it is unlikely — accept and hold a contingency reserve.'
'Escalation is for risks beyond your authority/scope — these four sit within the project.'

explanation: Match the response to the risk: Avoid a compliance breach, Mitigate a resource risk, Transfer a physical/insurable loss, Accept an unpreventable low-probability event. (Note: a regulation is Avoided/complied-with, never simply 'avoided by ignoring it', and a bankruptcy risk is usually Mitigated with a backup, not Transferred.)

prompt: A vendor's workaround bypasses critical testing to hit a deadline; the sponsor verbally accepts the risk. Put the two correct actions in order — drag one onto each step. Leave the wrong actions in the tray.

answers.why: 'First make the risk visible and analysed — you cannot decide on an undocumented, unquantified risk.'

answers.text: 'Route it through formal change control before proceeding'
answers.why: 'Only proceed after formal authorization — a verbal OK is not sufficient when significant risk/compliance is involved.'

answers.text: 'Proceed now — the sponsor approved it verbally'
answers.why: 'Verbal approval is not authorization; acting on it bypasses governance.'

answers.text: 'Implement now and fix the records later'
answers.why: 'Never bypass the process and back-fill documentation — that defeats change control.'

explanation: Document and quantify the risk FIRST, then obtain formal authorization through change control. A verbal sponsor OK never substitutes for Integrated Change Control when significant risk or compliance is at stake.
